quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Point mutation"?

options.text: Differential survival and reproduction of individuals with favorable heritable traits in a given environment; the primary mechanism of adaptive evolution
Species with high reproductive rates, short lifespans, and little parental care (e.g., bacteria, insects); adapted to unstable environments
A change in a single nucleotide base pair; includes substitutions (transitions, transversions) and single base insertions/deletions
Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., Iᴬ Iᴮ blood type produces both A and B antigens)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Point mutation" is defined as: A change in a single nucleotide base pair; includes substitutions (transitions, transversions) and single base insertions/deletions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Missense mutation"?

options.text: Multiple genes contribute to a single trait, producing continuous variation (e.g., skin color, height, intelligence)
ATP production driven by the proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane; H⁺ flows through ATP synthase from lumen to stroma
A nucleotide change that alters one amino acid in the protein; effects range from none to severe loss of function
The 4-carbon molecule that accepts acetyl-CoA to begin each turn of the Krebs cycle; regenerated at the end of each turn

quiz_questions.explanation: "Missense mutation" is defined as: A nucleotide change that alters one amino acid in the protein; effects range from none to severe loss of function. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nonsense mutation"?

options.text: The number of individuals contributing genetically to the next generation; usually much smaller than census size due to unequal sex ratios, variance in reproductive success
A molecule resembling the substrate that competes for the active site; increases apparent Km but does not affect Vmax (can be overcome by more substrate)
A nucleotide change that creates a premature stop codon, producing a truncated, usually nonfunctional protein
Species with low reproductive rates, long lifespans, and extensive parental care (e.g., elephants, whales); adapted to stable environments

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nonsense mutation" is defined as: A nucleotide change that creates a premature stop codon, producing a truncated, usually nonfunctional protein. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Frameshift mutation"?

options.text: 5' cap (7-methylguanosine) and 3' poly-A tail added for stability and export; introns spliced out by spliceosomes
The proportion of each genotype in a population: p² (AA) + 2pq (Aa) + q² (aa) = 1 under Hardy-Weinberg conditions
Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids
Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap

quiz_questions.explanation: "Frameshift mutation" is defined as: Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Germline vs. somatic mutation"?

options.text: Germline mutations (in eggs/sperm) are passed to offspring; somatic mutations affect only the individual and are not inherited
Non-native organisms that spread aggressively in new environments, outcompeting native species (e.g., kudzu, cane toads, zebra mussels)
The monomer of nucleic acids: a 5-carbon sugar (deoxyribose or ribose), a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
2pq; the proportion of heterozygous carriers of a recessive allele in a population, estimable from disease frequency (q²)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Germline vs. somatic mutation" is defined as: Germline mutations (in eggs/sperm) are passed to offspring; somatic mutations affect only the individual and are not inherited. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A change in a single nucleotide base pair; includes substitutions (transitions, transversions) and single base insertions/deletions"?

options.text: Leading strand
Mutagen
Point mutation
Central dogma

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Point mutation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A nucleotide change that alters one amino acid in the protein; effects range from none to severe loss of function"?

options.text: Ribosome
Net primary productivity (NPP)
Missense mutation
Nucleus

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Missense mutation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A nucleotide change that creates a premature stop codon, producing a truncated, usually nonfunctional protein"?

options.text: Molecular clock
Allele frequency
Nonsense mutation
Species-area relationship

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nonsense mutation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids"?

options.text: Tertiary structure
Frameshift mutation
Energy coupling
Carrying capacity (K)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Frameshift mutation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Germline mutations (in eggs/sperm) are passed to offspring; somatic mutations affect only the individual and are not inherited"?

options.text: Germline vs. somatic mutation
Phospholipid
Outgroup
Activation energy (Ea)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Germline vs. somatic mutation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Mutations and Genetic Variation is accurate?

options.text: Evolution and Evidence establishes that populations change over time through multiple converging lines of evidence.
Photosystems II and I absorb photons, exciting electrons that pass through electron transport chains.
The specific bond orientation (α vs. β linkages) determines digestibility and function: α-1,4 bonds in starch are digestible by humans, while β-1,4 bonds in cellulose are not.
Mutations and Genetic Variation are changes in DNA sequence that provide the raw material for evolution.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mutations and Genetic Variation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Mutations and Genetic Variation?

options.text: Alternative splicing enables one gene to produce multiple protein variants.
Point mutations include silent, missense, nonsense, and frameshift types, with varying effects on protein function.
Speciation occurs through geographic isolation (allopatric) or reproductive isolation within the same area (sympatric).

Eukaryotic cells compartmentalize functions in membrane-bound organelles: the nucleus (DNA storage), endoplasmic reticulum (protein/lipid synthesis), Golgi apparatus (modification and sorting), mitochondria (ATP production), and chloroplasts...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mutations and Genetic Variation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Mutations and Genetic Variation?

options.text: Parsimony (fewest evolutionary changes) and maximum likelihood methods are used to determine the most probable tree topology.
Saturated vs. unsaturated fatty acids differ in the presence of C=C double bonds, affecting fluidity and melting point.
Mutations can be spontaneous (replication errors) or induced by mutagens.
Nutrients (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water) cycle between biotic and abiotic components.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mutations and Genetic Variation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Mutations and Genetic Variation?

options.text: Phylogenies reveal the nested hierarchy of life and enable predictions about uncharacterized organisms.
While most mutations are neutral or harmful, some confer advantages (e.g., sickle cell heterozygote advantage against malaria).
Triglycerides (fats and oils) store energy (9 kcal/g) through ester bonds between glycerol and three fatty acids.
Monohybrid crosses (Aa × Aa → 3:1 phenotypic ratio) and dihybrid crosses (AaBb × AaBb → 9:3:3:1 ratio) predict offspring ratios, testable with chi-square analysis.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mutations and Genetic Variation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Mutations and Genetic Variation?

options.text: ATP hydrolysis (ΔG = -30.5 kJ/mol) couples to endergonic reactions to drive cellular work.
Mutations in somatic cells affect only the individual; germline mutations are heritable.
Conservation Biology integrates ecology, genetics, and evolutionary biology to protect and restore biological diversity.
The Knudson two-hit hypothesis explains that both copies of a tumor suppressor must be inactivated for cancer to develop.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mutations and Genetic Variation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Mutations and Genetic Variation?

options.text: Sickle cell trait (HbAS heterozygotes) conferring malaria resistance while homozygotes (HbSS) suffer sickle cell disease—a classic example of heterozygote advantage
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence
Telomere shortening with each cell division limiting cell lifespan; telomerase activation in cancer cells enables unlimited replication (immortality)
Evaporative cooling in humans through sweating, utilizing water's high heat of vaporization (2,260 J/g) to dissipate excess body heat

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mutations and Genetic Variation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Mutations and Genetic Variation?

options.text: Blood glucose regulation via insulin and glucagon controlling the interconversion of glucose and glycogen in liver and muscle cells
BRCA1/2 germline mutations increasing breast cancer risk to 60-80% lifetime probability, enabling preventive screening and treatment decisions

Epinephrine (adrenaline) signaling cascade: one epinephrine molecule activates many G-proteins, each activating adenylyl cyclase producing millions of cAMP molecules, amplifying the fight-or-flight response
Virus tracking during COVID-19 pandemic using SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing to build phylogenetic trees tracing transmission chains and identifying new variants

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mutations and Genetic Variation.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Mutations and Genetic Variation applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Lac operon as a model for understanding antibiotic resistance gene regulation in bacteria, where environmental signals trigger expression of resistance genes
Genetic engineering using the universal genetic code to express human insulin in E. coli bacteria, producing recombinant insulin for diabetes treatment
Antibiotic resistance emerging through bacterial mutations that survive selection pressure, creating resistant strains like MRSA
Capillary action in plant xylem vessels transporting water against gravity from roots to leaves through cohesion-adhesion-tension

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mutations and Genetic Variation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Mutations and Genetic Variation?

options.text: Sickle cell trait (HbAS heterozygotes) conferring malaria resistance while homozygotes (HbSS) suffer sickle cell disease—a classic example of heterozygote advantage
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence
Virus tracking during COVID-19 pandemic using SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing to build phylogenetic trees tracing transmission chains and identifying new variants
Coral bleaching when heat stress damages photosystem proteins in symbiotic zooxanthellae, disrupting photosynthesis and causing coral death

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mutations and Genetic Variation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Mutations and Genetic Variation in practice?

options.text: BRCA1/2 germline mutations increasing breast cancer risk to 60-80% lifetime probability, enabling preventive screening and treatment decisions
Artificial photosynthesis research mimicking PSII's water-splitting capability to generate hydrogen fuel using sunlight
Artificial selection in agriculture: selective breeding transforming teosinte (small, hard kernels) into modern corn over thousands of years of human-directed selection
Blood glucose regulation via insulin and glucagon controlling the interconversion of glucose and glycogen in liver and muscle cells

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mutations and Genetic Variation.
